name: AspectTableMapping
description:
Maps an AspectDef to a database table, with property-to-column mappings.

This class enables aspects to be stored in and loaded from custom database tables rather than the standard aspect/property_value tables.

Table Structure Patterns

The mapping supports four different table structure patterns based on the presence of catalog_id and entity_id columns:

  • No IDs (hasCatalogId=false, hasEntityId=false): Generic lookup table with no primary key. Table is truncated before each save. Entity IDs are generated on load.
  • Catalog ID only (hasCatalogId=true, hasEntityId=false): Catalog-scoped table with no primary key. Rows for the catalog are deleted before save. Entity IDs are generated on load.
  • Entity ID only (hasCatalogId=false, hasEntityId=true): Entity-scoped table with PRIMARY KEY (entity_id). Standard behavior with INSERT...ON CONFLICT. Entity IDs are pre

name: CheapTestFactory
description:
Factory class for creating common test data used across cheap-db test classes.
This class helps reduce duplication in test setup by providing reusable methods
for creating and populating test Cheap elements and database tables.

name: JdbcCatalogBase
description:
Base class for Catalog implementations that load database tables via JDBC.
Provides common functionality for mapping database schemas to Cheap AspectDefs
and loading table data into AspectMapHierarchies.

name: MariaDbCatalog
description:
Catalog implementation that loads MariaDB database tables as Cheap AspectMapHierarchies.

This class extends {@link JdbcCatalogBase} to provide MariaDB-specific database metadata access and type mapping. It introspects MariaDB database schemas to automatically create AspectDefs from table definitions, and loads table data as aspects within the catalog.

Each table in the MariaDB database is represented as an AspectDef with properties corresponding to the table's columns. The table data is loaded into an AspectMapHierarchy where each row becomes an Aspect attached to an Entity.

MariaDB-specific characteristics handled by this class:

  • Schema support (defaults to "public" schema)
  • Rich type system including UUID, JSON, arrays, geometric types, etc.
  • Metadata accessed via information_schema.tables
  • Strong type checking with custom type conversions
  • Support for serial/bigserial auto-increment types

name: MariaDbCheapSchema
MariaDbDao
description:
Data Access Object for persisting and loading complete Catalog instances to/from a MariaDB database using the Cheap schema.

This DAO provides comprehensive persistence capabilities for the entire Cheap data model, including catalogs, aspect definitions, hierarchies, entities, aspects, and properties. It supports transactional saves and loads with full referential integrity.

Key Features

  • Full Model Persistence: Saves/loads complete catalog structures including all definitions, entities, hierarchies, aspects, and properties
  • Transaction Management: All save operations execute within transactions with automatic rollback on failure
  • Two Persistence Modes: Supports both default schema tables and custom table mappings via {@link AspectTableMapping}
  • DDL Management: Provides methods to create, audit, and drop the Cheap database schema
  • CheapFactory Integratio

name: SqliteCatalog
description:
Catalog implementation that loads SQLite database tables as Cheap AspectMapHierarchies.

This class extends {@link JdbcCatalogBase} to provide SQLite-specific database metadata access and type mapping. It introspects SQLite database schemas to automatically create AspectDefs from table definitions, and loads table data as aspects within the catalog.

Each table in the SQLite database is represented as an AspectDef with properties corresponding to the table's columns. The table data is loaded into an AspectMapHierarchy where each row becomes an Aspect attached to an Entity.

SQLite-specific characteristics handled by this class:

  • No schema names (SQLite doesn't use schemas)
  • Type affinity system where column types map to property types
  • Metadata accessed via sqlite_master system table
  • Dynamic typing with TEXT, INTEGER, REAL, BLOB, and NUMERIC affinities

name: SqliteCheapSchema
SqliteDao
description:
Data Access Object for persisting and loading complete Catalog instances to/from a SQLite database using the Cheap schema.

This DAO provides comprehensive persistence capabilities for the entire Cheap data model, including catalogs, aspect definitions, hierarchies, entities, aspects, and properties. It supports transactional saves and loads with full referential integrity.

SQLite-Specific Considerations

  • UUID Storage: UUIDs are stored as TEXT (36-character strings)
  • Boolean Storage: Booleans are stored as INTEGER (0 or 1)
  • Timestamp Storage: Timestamps are stored as TEXT in ISO8601 format
  • Foreign Keys: Must be explicitly enabled per connection
@see CheapDao @see AspectTableMapping @see CheapFactory @see Catalog

name: AspectBaseImpl
AspectBuilderBase
description:
Abstract base class providing common functionality for AspectBuilder implementations. This class handles entity management, aspect definition storage, property collection, and validation logic that is shared across different builder implementations.

The base class maintains state for the entity, aspect definition, and properties, and provides template methods for subclasses to customize the aspect creation process while reusing common validation and property management logic.

Subclasses must implement {@link #createAspect()} to provide the specific logic for creating the appropriate aspect type with the configured entity, aspect definition, and properties.

Key features provided by this base class:

  • Entity and aspect definition management with validation
  • Property collection with both name-value and Property object support
  • Standard validation for build prerequisites
  • Reset functionality for builder reuse</

name: AspectObjectMapBuilder
description:
Builder implementation for creating AspectObjectMapImpl instances using the builder pattern. This class provides a fluent interface for configuring and building aspects with property values.

This implementation extends {@link AspectBuilderBase} to inherit common builder functionality while providing specific logic for creating {@link AspectObjectMapImpl} instances.

Example usage:

{@code
AspectObjectMapBuilder builder = new AspectObjectMapBuilder();
Aspect aspect = builder
    .entity(myEntity)
    .aspectDef(myAspectDef)
    .property("name", "John Doe")
    .property("age", 30)
    .build();
}

name: AspectObjectMapImpl
AspectPropertyMapBuilder
description:
Builder implementation for creating AspectPropertyMapImpl instances using the builder pattern. This class provides a fluent interface for configuring and building aspects with Property objects.

This builder enforces strict validation against the AspectDef, ensuring that all properties are defined in the aspect definition and that property values are compatible with their types. PropertyDef objects are only taken from the aspectDef, never constructed.

The builder maintains state for the entity, aspect definition, and properties until {@link #build()} is called, at which point it creates and returns an AspectPropertyMapImpl instance with all configured values.

Example usage:

{@code
AspectPropertyMapBuilder builder = new AspectPropertyMapBuilder();
Aspect aspect = builder
    .entity(entity)
    .aspectDef(myAspectDef)
    .property("name", "John Doe")
    .property("age", 30)
    .build();
}

This factory can be configured with a default LocalEntityType to control which implementation is used when creating LocalEntity instances.

This factory simplifies object creation and provides a clean API for instantiating Cheap model objects without directly depending on implementation classes.

name: CheapReflectionUtil
description:
Utility class providing reflection-based operations and type mapping functionality for the Cheap reflection package.

This class serves as the core utility for all reflection-based implementations in the Cheap system. It provides essential functionality for:

  • Java type to {@link PropertyType} mapping
  • Getter and setter method validation
  • Nullability inference from annotations
  • Multivalued property detection (arrays and collections)
  • Type analysis for generic types and parameterized types

Type Mapping:

The class maintains a comprehensive mapping from Java types to Cheap {@link PropertyType} values, including support for:

  • Primitive types and their boxed equivalents
  • String and character sequence types
  • Numeric types (integers, floats, BigInteger)
  • Date and time types (Date, Calendar, TemporalAccessor)
  • URI and URL type

name: ImmutablePojoAspect
description:
An {@link Aspect} implementation that provides read-only access to Plain Old Java Objects (POJOs) through the Cheap property model.

This class wraps a POJO instance and exposes its JavaBean properties as Cheap properties using the property definitions from an {@link ImmutablePojoAspectDef}. It enforces immutability by providing read-only access to properties and throwing {@link UnsupportedOperationException} for any write, add, or remove operations, regardless of whether the underlying POJO has setter methods.

Key features:

  • Read-only property access through JavaBean getter methods
  • Type-safe POJO instance storage with generic type parameter
  • Integration with Cheap's entity and catalog system
  • Efficient property value retrieval using cached method wrappers
  • Immutability enforcement at the aspect level

name: ImmutablePojoAspectBuilder
description:
Builder implementation for creating ImmutablePojoAspect instances using the builder pattern. This class provides a fluent interface for configuring and building immutable POJO aspects with property values.

This builder extends {@link AspectBuilderBase} to inherit common builder functionality while providing specific logic for creating {@link ImmutablePojoAspect} instances. The builder creates immutable POJO aspects that provide read-only access to JavaBean properties through reflection-based getter methods.

Unlike mutable POJO aspects, the properties set through this builder are used to initialize the underlying POJO instance via reflection on setter methods during construction, but the resulting aspect enforces immutability by throwing {@link UnsupportedOperationException} for any subsequent write operations.

name: ImmutablePojoAspectDef
description:
An {@link net.netbeing.cheap.model.AspectDef} implementation for immutable Plain Old Java Objects (POJOs) that provides read-only access to properties through JavaBean-style getter methods.

This class uses Java's standard introspection mechanism ({@link Introspector}) to discover JavaBean properties in a POJO class and creates corresponding {@link PojoPropertyDef} instances. All properties are treated as read-only regardless of whether setter methods exist in the class, enforcing immutable semantics at the aspect level.

Key features:

  • Automatic property discovery using Java Bean introspection
  • Read-only property definitions ignoring setter methods
  • Cached getter method wrappers for efficient property access
  • Integration with Cheap's reflection utilities for type mapping

JavaBean property discovery:

  • Uses {@link Introspector#getBeanInfo(Class,Class)} to find properties

name: MutablePojoAspect
description:
An {@link Aspect} implementation that provides full read-write access to Plain Old Java Objects (POJOs) through the Cheap property model.

This class wraps a POJO instance and exposes its JavaBean properties as Cheap properties using the property definitions from a {@link MutablePojoAspectDef}. Unlike {@link ImmutablePojoAspect}, this implementation supports both reading and writing properties through their respective getter and setter methods when available.

Key features:

  • Full read-write property access through JavaBean getter and setter methods
  • Type-safe POJO instance storage with generic type parameter
  • Integration with Cheap's entity and catalog system
  • Efficient property value access using cached method wrappers
  • Intelligent primitive type handling for proper boxing/unboxing

Property access behavior:

  • Read operations use cached {@link GenericGetterSetter} getter wrapp

name: MutablePojoAspectBuilder
MutablePojoAspectDef
description:
An {@link net.netbeing.cheap.model.AspectDef} implementation for mutable Plain Old Java Objects (POJOs) that provides full read-write access to properties through JavaBean-style getter and setter methods. Unlike {@link MutableAspectDefImpl}, this implementation does NOT allow addition or removal of properties.

This class uses Java's standard introspection mechanism ({@link Introspector}) to discover JavaBean properties in a POJO class and creates corresponding {@link PojoPropertyDef} instances. Unlike {@link ImmutablePojoAspectDef}, this class preserves both getter and setter methods, allowing full mutation capabilities for properties that have both accessor types.

Key features:

  • Automatic property discovery using Java Bean introspection
  • Full read-write property definitions respecting both getters and setters
  • Cached getter and setter method wrappers for efficient property access
  • Support for read-only, write-only,

name: RecordAspect

This class wraps a Java record instance and exposes its components as Cheap properties using the property definitions from a {@link RecordAspectDef}. It maintains the immutability semantics of Java records by throwing {@link UnsupportedOperationException} for any write, add, or remove operations.

Key features:

  • Read-only property access through record component accessor methods
  • Type-safe record instance storage with generic type parameter
  • Integration with Cheap's entity and catalog system
  • Efficient property value retrieval using cached method wrappers

name: RecordAspectBuilder
description:
Builder implementation for creating RecordAspect instances using the builder pattern. This class provides a fluent interface for configuring and building record aspects with property values that correspond to record components.

This builder extends {@link AspectBuilderBase} to inherit common builder functionality while providing specific logic for creating {@link RecordAspect} instances. Since Java records are immutable by design, the builder creates record instances by calling the canonical constructor with the configured property values, then wraps the record in an immutable aspect.

The builder automatically discovers the record's components and their types, then matches configured properties to constructor parameters by name and position. Properties must be set for all record components that don't have default values.

Example usage:

This class bridges Java record types to the Cheap model by introspecting record components and creating corresponding {@link RecordPropertyDef} instances. It provides read-only access to record data through generated property definitions and cached accessor methods.

Key features:

  • Automatic aspect definition generation from {@link RecordComponent} metadata
  • Immutable property definitions reflecting Java record immutability
  • Cached method resolution for efficient property access
  • Seamless integration with Cheap's reflection utilities

name: AbstractRestClientIntegrationTest
description:
Abstract base class for REST client integration tests with common test methods.
Contains @Test methods that verify common functionality across all database backends.

Database-specific test classes should extend this to inherit all common tests
and add their own database-specific tests.

This is separate from BaseClientIntegrationTest to allow CrossDatabaseConsistencyTest
to extend BaseClientIntegrationTest without inheriting these @Test methods.

name: BaseClientIntegrationTest

Provides common utilities for REST client access and test data generation.

Subclasses must configure their own Spring context with @SpringBootTest.
All tests interact ONLY through REST client - NO direct database access.

name: SqliteRestIntegrationTest

Base class for integration tests using SQLite database.

name: CrossDatabaseConsistencyTest
description:
Cross-database consistency tests.
Verifies that the same operations produce consistent results across all three database backends.

Architecture:
- Starts three separate Spring Boot servers (PostgreSQL on 8081, SQLite on 8082, MariaDB on 8083)
- Creates three separate CheapRestClient instances (one for each server)
- NO direct database access - ALL verification through REST API responses only
- Tests perform identical operations on all three clients and compare results

name: MariaDbDockerIntegrationTest

Docker-based integration test for MariaDB backend.

Architecture:
- Client test runs in JVM process (NOT in Docker)
- Server (cheap-rest) runs in Docker container
- MariaDB runs in Docker container
- Test ONLY uses CheapRestClient (NO database access)

name: MultiDatabaseDockerOrchestrationTest
description:
Multi-container orchestration test that runs all three database backends simultaneously.

Architecture:
- Client test runs in JVM process (NOT in Docker)
- Three separate cheap-rest servers run in Docker containers
- Three database containers (PostgreSQL, MariaDB, SQLite)
- Test uses three separate CheapRestClient instances

This test verifies:
- All three backends can run simultaneously without conflicts
- Consistent behavior across all database backends
- Network isolation between services

name: AspectDefDeserializer
description:
Jackson deserializer for {@link AspectDef} objects in the Cheap data model.

This deserializer reconstructs an AspectDef from JSON format, including its name, access control flags, and property definitions. The deserializer intelligently selects the appropriate AspectDef implementation (ImmutableAspectDef, MutableAspectDef, or FullAspectDef) based on the mutability flags in the JSON.

The deserializer handles both aspect-level and property-level flags, with property flags inheriting aspect defaults when not explicitly specified. This allows for compact JSON representation while maintaining full configuration flexibility.

After deserialization, the AspectDef is automatically registered with the CheapFactory to make it available for subsequent AspectMapHierarchy deserialization. If an AspectDef with the same name already exists, the deserializer verifies full equality to prevent conflicts.

This class is package-private and used internally b

name: AspectMapDeserializer
description:
Jackson deserializer for {@link AspectMap} objects in the Cheap data model.

Deserializes entity-to-aspect mappings, using context attributes to pass AspectDef and Entity to the AspectDeserializer

Entity references are resolved through the CheapFactory's entity registry, ensuring that entities with the same UUID are represented by the same Entity object instances.

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CheapJacksonDeserializer} and {@link CatalogDeserializer}.

@see AspectMap

name: CatalogDefDeserializer
CatalogDeserializer
description:
Jackson deserializer for {@link Catalog} objects in the Cheap data model.

This deserializer reconstructs a Catalog from JSON format, creating all necessary AspectDef and Hierarchy objects and populating them with data from the JSON structure. The deserializer uses a {@link CheapFactory} to create model objects consistently.

The deserialization process follows a specific order:

  1. Parse catalog metadata (globalId, URI, species, upstream, version)
  2. Deserialize and register all AspectDefs with the factory
  3. Store hierarchy JSON data for deferred processing
  4. Create the Catalog instance
  5. Deserialize hierarchies (which can now resolve AspectDef references)

This two-phase approach ensures that AspectDef objects are available when deserializing AspectMapHierarchy contents, avoiding forward reference issues.

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CheapJacksonDeseria

name: CheapJacksonDeserializer
description:
Jackson-based JSON deserializer for Cheap data model objects. Provides deserialization capabilities using Jackson with custom deserializers that can be configured with a CheapFactory for object creation.

This class can be used in two modes:

  • Default mode: Uses a static ObjectMapper with a default CheapFactory
  • Factory mode: Creates a new ObjectMapper configured with the provided CheapFactory

Uses custom Jackson deserializers for each Cheap element type that leverage the CheapFactory for consistent object creation.

name: HierarchyDefDeserializer
description:
Jackson deserializer for {@link HierarchyDef} objects in the Cheap data model.

This deserializer reconstructs a HierarchyDef from JSON format by reading its name and type code. HierarchyDef is a simple metadata structure that specifies the name and organizational type of a hierarchy.

The deserializer converts two-character type codes (EL, ES, ED, ET, AM) to the corresponding {@link HierarchyType} enumeration values. Legacy fields like "isModifiable" are gracefully ignored for backward compatibility.

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CatalogDefDeserializer} when deserializing catalog definitions from JSON.

@see HierarchyDef @see HierarchyType @see CatalogDefDeserializer @see CheapFactory

name: HierarchyDeserializer
description:
Jackson deserializer for {@link Hierarchy} objects in the Cheap data model.

This deserializer reconstructs any type of Hierarchy from JSON format by dispatching to specialized deserialization methods based on the hierarchy type code (EL, ES, ED, ET, or AM). The deserializer uses a {@link CheapFactory} to create hierarchy instances and manage entity references consistently.

Each hierarchy type has a corresponding deserialization method:

  • {@link AspectMapHierarchy}: Deserializes entity-to-aspect mappings, using context attributes to pass AspectDef and Entity to the AspectDeserializer
  • {@link EntityDirectoryHierarchy}: Deserializes string-to-entity mappings
  • {@link EntityListHierarchy}: Deserializes ordered entity UUID arrays
  • {@link EntitySetHierarchy}: Deserializes unique entity UUID arrays
  • {@link EntityTreeHierarchy}: Deserializes recursive tree structures with TreeNodeDeserializer
<

name: PropertyDefDeserializer
description:
Jackson deserializer for {@link PropertyDef} objects in the Cheap data model.

This deserializer reconstructs a PropertyDef from JSON format, including its name, type, default value (if any), and all configuration flags (isReadable, isWritable, isNullable, isRemovable, isMultivalued).

PropertyDef objects are typically deserialized as part of an AspectDef, where they are embedded inline within the "propertyDefs" array. However, this deserializer can also handle standalone PropertyDef deserialization if needed.

The deserializer uses the {@link CheapFactory#createPropertyDef} method to ensure consistent PropertyDef creation with proper defaults and validation.

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link AspectDefDeserializer} when deserializing AspectDef property collections.

@see PropertyDef @see AspectDefDeserializer @see CheapFactory

name: TreeNodeDeserializer
description:
Jackson deserializer for {@link Node} objects in the Cheap data model.

This deserializer recursively reconstructs tree node structures from JSON format, handling both leaf nodes and internal nodes with children. Each node can optionally contain an entity reference identified by UUID.

The deserializer processes JSON with the following structure:

  • entityId: Optional UUID of the entity associated with this node
  • isLeaf: Optional flag indicating if this is a leaf node (defaults to false)
  • children: Optional object mapping string keys to child nodes (for non-leaf nodes)

Entity references are resolved through the CheapFactory's entity registry to ensure consistent Entity object instances across the deserialization process. The deserializer uses the factory to create the appropriate node type (leaf or internal) based on the isLeaf flag.

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link Hiera

name: AspectDefSerializer
description:
Jackson serializer for {@link AspectDef} objects in the Cheap data model.

This serializer converts an AspectDef to JSON format, including its name, access control flags (isReadable, isWritable, canAddProperties, canRemoveProperties), and the collection of PropertyDef specifications.

The serializer optimizes the JSON output by omitting boolean flags that have their default values (true for isReadable/isWritable, false for canAddProperties/canRemoveProperties). This reduces JSON verbosity while maintaining full fidelity.

Property definitions are serialized inline as an array of objects, with each PropertyDef containing its name, type, and any non-default configuration flags.

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CheapJacksonSerializer} when serializing Catalog objects that contain AspectDef collections.

@see AspectDef @see PropertyDef @see CatalogSerializer

name: AspectMapSerializer
AspectSerializer
description:
Jackson serializer for {@link Aspect} objects in the Cheap data model.

This serializer converts an Aspect to JSON format, including all of its properties as defined by its AspectDef. The serializer can optionally include the aspect definition name and entity ID in the output based on configuration flags.

The serializer iterates through all property definitions in the aspect's AspectDef and writes the corresponding property values to JSON. Only non-null property values are included in the output to keep the JSON concise.

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CheapJacksonSerializer} and {@link HierarchySerializer} when serializing AspectMapHierarchy contents.

@see Aspect @see AspectDef @see CheapJacksonSerializer

name: CatalogDefSerializer
description:
Jackson serializer for {@link CatalogDef} objects in the Cheap data model.

This serializer converts a CatalogDef to JSON format, including its collections of AspectDef and HierarchyDef objects. A CatalogDef is purely informational and defines the structure of data types that a catalog typically contains.

The serializer outputs AspectDefs as an object (map) keyed by aspect name, and HierarchyDefs as an array. This structure matches the lookup patterns commonly used when working with catalog definitions.

This class is package-private and may be used internally by {@link CheapJacksonSerializer} for standalone CatalogDef serialization or as part of metadata exports.

@see CatalogDef @see AspectDef @see HierarchyDef @see CheapJacksonSerializer

name: CatalogSerializer
description:
Jackson serializer for {@link Catalog} objects in the Cheap data model.

This serializer converts a Catalog to JSON format, including its global ID, URI, species, upstream catalog reference, aspect definitions, and all contained hierarchies. The output JSON structure provides a complete representation of the catalog suitable for storage or transmission.

The serializer produces JSON with the following structure: 

{@code
{
  "globalId": "uuid-string",
  "uri": "catalog-uri" or null,
  "species": "source|sink|mirror|cache|clone|fork",
  "upstream": "upstream-catalog-uuid" (optional),
  "aspectDefs": {
    "aspectDefName": { AspectDef JSON... },
    ...
  },
  "hierarchies": {
    "hierarchyName": { Hierarchy JSON... },
    ...
  }
}
}

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CheapJacksonSerializer}. It delegates to other specialized serializers for nested objects like AspectDef and Hierarchy.

@see Catal

name: CheapJacksonSerializer
description:
Jackson-based JSON serializer for Cheap data model objects. Provides the same functionality as CheapJsonRawSerializer but uses Jackson for more flexible and extensible JSON generation.

This class provides methods for serializing Cheap model objects to JSON format, as defined by the JSON schemas in this module.

Uses custom Jackson serializers for each Cheap element type since no Jackson annotations are used on the model classes.

name: HierarchyDefSerializer
description:
Jackson serializer for {@link HierarchyDef} objects in the Cheap data model.

This serializer converts a HierarchyDef to JSON format, including its name and type code. HierarchyDef is a simple structure that specifies the metadata for a hierarchy, defining its name and organizational type (EL, ES, ED, ET, or AM).

The serializer produces compact JSON with just two fields:

  • name: The unique name of the hierarchy within its catalog
  • type: The two-character type code (EL, ES, ED, ET, or AM)

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CatalogDefSerializer} when serializing catalog definitions, or by {@link CheapJacksonSerializer} for standalone hierarchy definition serialization.

@see HierarchyDef @see HierarchyType @see CatalogDefSerializer

name: HierarchySerializer
description:
Jackson serializer for {@link Hierarchy} objects in the Cheap data model.

This serializer converts any Hierarchy to JSON format by dispatching to specialized serialization methods based on the hierarchy type (ENTITY_LIST, ENTITY_SET, ENTITY_DIR, ENTITY_TREE, or ASPECT_MAP).

Each hierarchy type has a corresponding serialization method that produces JSON appropriate for its structure:

  • {@link AspectMapHierarchy}: Object mapping entity UUIDs to Aspect objects
  • {@link EntityDirectoryHierarchy}: Object mapping string keys to entity UUIDs
  • {@link EntityListHierarchy}: Array of entity UUIDs (may contain duplicates)
  • {@link EntitySetHierarchy}: Array of unique entity UUIDs
  • {@link EntityTreeHierarchy}: Recursive tree structure with entity values

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CheapJacksonSerializer} when serializing Catalog objects that contain Hierarchy collection

name: PropertyDefSerializer
description:
Jackson serializer for {@link PropertyDef} objects in the Cheap data model.

This serializer converts a PropertyDef to JSON format, including its name, type, and various configuration flags.

The serializer optimizes the JSON output by omitting boolean flags that have their default values to reduce JSON verbosity while maintaining full fidelity.

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CheapJacksonSerializer} when serializing standalone PropertyDef objects.

@see PropertyDef

name: PropertySerializer
description:
Jackson serializer for {@link Property} objects in the Cheap data model.

This serializer converts a Property to JSON format, including its definition and value.

The serialized format includes:

  • def: The PropertyDef that defines this property's type and constraints
  • value: The actual value of the property (may be null)

This class is package-private and used internally by {@link CheapJacksonSerializer} when serializing standalone Property objects.

@see Property

    "john.doe@example.com"
);

Entity appEntity = factory.createEntity(catalog, UUID.randomUUID());
TagApplicationAspect aspect = new TagApplicationAspect(appEntity, application);

This class provides a CHEAP-native representation of tag definitions, wrapping the TagDefinition POJO with the ImmutablePojoAspect framework. It uses reflection-based property access for efficient field retrieval and provides convenience methods for commonly accessed properties.

TagDefinitionAspect instances are immutable and provide read-only access to the underlying TagDefinition POJO through the CHEAP property model.

Example usage:

Entity tagEntity = factory.createEntity(catalog, UUID.randomUUID());

name: TagApplication
description:
Records the application of a tag to a specific CHEAP element.

A tag application captures:

  • Which tag definition is being applied
  • Which element is being tagged
  • When and how the tag was applied
  • Optional tag-specific metadata

Tag applications are immutable value objects. Once created, their properties cannot be changed. To modify a tag application, remove it and create a new one with the desired properties.

Example usage:

{@code
TagApplication application = new TagApplication(
    invoiceTagId,                           // tagDefinitionId
    propertyId,                             // targetElementId
    ElementType.PROPERTY,                   // targetType
    Map.of("format", "INV-{year}-{seq}"),  // metadata
    TagSource.EXPLICIT,                     // source
    ZonedDateTime.now(),                    // appliedAt
    "john.doe@example.com"                  // appliedBy
);
}</

name: TagDefinition
description:
Defines a tag type that can be applied to CHEAP elements.

A tag definition specifies:

  • A qualified name ({@code namespace.name}) that uniquely identifies the tag
  • Which element types the tag can be applied to
  • Whether it's a standard or custom tag
  • Optional parent tags for inheritance hierarchies
  • Optional aliases for alternative names

Tag definitions are immutable value objects. Once created, their properties cannot be changed. To modify a tag definition, create a new one with the desired properties.

Example usage:

{@code
TagDefinition invoiceTag = new TagDefinition(
    "myapp.domain",                          // namespace
    "invoice-number",                        // name
    "Identifies invoice documents uniquely", // description
    List.of(ElementType.PROPERTY),          // appliesTo
    TagScope.CUSTOM,                        // scope
    List.of("invoice-id"),

name: TagQuery
description:
Fluent query builder for searching elements by tags.

Provides a rich API for constructing complex tag-based queries with multiple filters and criteria. Supports filtering by:

  • Element type (Property, Aspect, Hierarchy, Entity, Catalog)
  • Required tags (elements must have all specified tags)
  • Excluded tags (elements must not have any specified tags)
  • Namespace inclusion/exclusion
  • Tag source (explicit, inferred, generated)
  • Inherited tags (include or exclude tags from parent definitions)

Example usage:

{@code
TagQuery query = new TagQuery(registry)
    .forType(ElementType.PROPERTY)
    .withTagName("cheap.core", "pii")
    .withTagName("cheap.core", "encrypted")
    .inNamespace("myapp.security")
    .includeInheritedTags(true);

TagQueryResult result = query.execute();
for (UUID elementId : result.getElements()) {
    // Process PII properties that are encrypted
}

name: StandardTags
description:
Standard tag definitions in the "cheap.core" namespace.

This class provides a curated set of 60+ standard tags organized into semantic categories. These tags cover common metadata patterns for:

  • Identity and Keys (primary keys, foreign keys, etc.)
  • Temporal and Versioning (timestamps, version numbers, etc.)
  • Lifecycle and State (soft deletes, status flags, etc.)
  • Relationships (parent references, polymorphic links, etc.)
  • Data Semantics (display names, sort order, etc.)
  • Validation and Constraints (required, unique, immutable, etc.)
  • Security and Privacy (PII, encryption, audit logging, etc.)
  • Business Domain (monetary amounts, email addresses, etc.)
  • Technical Behavior (indexing, caching, lazy loading, etc.)

Usage:

{@code
// Get all standard tags
Collection allTags = StandardTags.allStandardTags();

// Get specific tag by

name: TagConflictDetector
description:
Detects semantic conflicts between tags applied to the same element.

This class identifies mutually exclusive or contradictory tags that should not be applied together. For example:

  • immutable + modified-timestamp (immutable data shouldn't track modifications)
  • required + nullable (a field can't be both required and nullable)
  • encrypted + masked (redundant privacy controls)

Conflict rules are defined as static mappings and can be extended in future versions to support custom conflict detection logic.

name: TagValidator
description:
Validates tag definitions and applications.

This class provides comprehensive validation for:

  • Tag definition format (namespace, name)
  • Tag application rules (applicability, existence)
  • Tag inheritance (parent existence, circular references)

Unit tests for the CheapFactory class.
Tests factory methods for creating instances of all Cheap model objects.

name: CheapFileUtil
description:
Utility class for loading filesystem data into Cheap catalogs.

This class provides methods to traverse filesystem directories and convert file/directory information into Cheap model structures. Files and directories are represented as {@link FileRec} record aspects and can be loaded into catalogs as either flat collections or hierarchical tree structures.

The utility supports various file traversal options including depth limits and symbolic link handling through standard NIO2 {@link FileVisitOption}s. @see FileRec @see EntityTreeHierarchy @see RecordAspect

name: CheapHasher
description:
Utility class for computing 64-bit FNV-1a hash values of various typed inputs.

The FNV-1a (Fowler-Noll-Vo) hash algorithm is a fast, non-cryptographic hash function that provides good distribution for hash table and checksum applications.

This class provides two modes of operation:

  • Static methods - Compute standalone hash values for individual inputs
  • Instance methods - Maintain a rolling hash that can be updated incrementally

Supports all Java classes used by PropertyType enum values:

  • Long (Integer type)
  • Double (Float type)
  • Boolean (Boolean type)
  • String (String, Text, CLOB types)
  • BigInteger (BigInteger type)
  • BigDecimal (BigDecimal type)
  • ZonedDateTime (DateTime type)
  • URI (URI type)
  • UUID (UUID type)
  • byte[] (BLOB type)

name: CheapHasherTest

Unit tests for the CheapHasher class.
Tests FNV-1a hash computation for all PropertyType javaClass types.

name: MethodDef
ReflectionWrapper
description:
Factory for creating optimized lambda-based implementations of reflection operations.

This class provides the core functionality for converting traditional reflection-based method calls into high-performance lambda implementations using the Java method handle and lambda metafactory infrastructure. It bridges the gap between target methods and the {@link GenericGetterSetter} interface by creating runtime lambda implementations that avoid the overhead of traditional reflection.

Key Features:

  • Signature-based matching between target methods and wrapper interface methods
  • Automatic lambda generation for type-safe method dispatch
  • Support for all primitive types without boxing overhead
  • Exception handling with automatic wrapping of checked exceptions

Performance Benefits: Lambda-based implementations created by this wrapper approach the performance of direct method calls after JIT optim

name: CatalogSpecies
description:
A Species is the fundamental type of a catalog in the Cheap data model. The species determines the data source relationship and caching behavior of the catalog within the overall system architecture.

All catalogs in Cheap are caches or working copies, but they differ in their relationship to upstream data sources and their write-through behavior.

name: HierarchyType
description:
Defines the different types of hierarchies supported in the Cheap data model. Each hierarchy type serves a specific organizational purpose and has unique characteristics for storing and accessing entities.

The hierarchy types follow the Cheap model's flexible approach to data organization, allowing entities to be structured in various ways depending on the use case.

name: LocalEntityType
description:
Enumeration of the different types of LocalEntity implementations available in the Cheap system. Each type corresponds to a specific implementation class with different performance characteristics and catalog management strategies.

This enum is used to configure factory methods to create the appropriate type of LocalEntity based on application needs.

@see LocalEntity

name: PropertyType
description:
Defines the supported data types for properties in the Cheap model. Each property type specifies storage characteristics, validation rules, and the corresponding Java class used for value representation.

Property types range from basic primitives to complex streaming types, providing comprehensive data storage capabilities while maintaining type safety and efficient serialization.

name: ArtifactType
description:
Types of code artifacts that can be extracted.
Values must match Python MetadataArtifact schema exactly.

name: SourceType
description:
Source types for metadata artifacts.
Values must match Python MetadataArtifact schema exactly.

name: ElementType
TagScope
description:
Defines the scope of a tag definition, distinguishing between standard built-in tags and custom user-defined tags.

Tag scopes provide semantic categorization:

  • STANDARD - Built-in tags provided by the CHEAP framework in the {@code cheap.core} namespace. These tags have well-defined semantics and are part of the framework's standard vocabulary.
  • CUSTOM - User-defined tags in application-specific namespaces. These tags allow applications to extend the tagging system with domain-specific metadata.

Standard tags are initialized by the TagRegistry and provide common metadata patterns such as primary keys, timestamps, and data semantics. Custom tags allow applications to define their own semantic markers tailored to specific business domains.

@see TagDefinition

name: TagSource
description:
Indicates how a tag application was created, distinguishing between manual, inferred, and automated tag applications.

Tag sources provide provenance tracking:

  • EXPLICIT - Manually applied by a user or developer. These tags represent intentional, human-made assertions about data semantics.
  • INFERRED - Derived by system rules or logic based on other metadata. These tags are automatically applied when certain conditions or patterns are detected.
  • GENERATED - Created by automated processes such as schema discovery, data profiling, or machine learning. These tags may require validation or review.

Source tracking enables filtering, auditing, and trust decisions based on how tags were applied. For example, explicitly-applied tags might be treated with higher confidence than generated tags.

@see TagApplication

name: CLASS_PROPERTY_TYPE_MAP
description:
Immutable mapping from Java types to their corresponding Cheap {@link PropertyType} values.

This map provides efficient type resolution for commonly used Java types. The mapping includes:

  • Boolean types: boolean, Boolean → PropertyType.Boolean
  • Floating point types: float, Float, double, Double → PropertyType.Float
  • Integer types: byte, Byte, short, Short, int, Integer, long, Long → PropertyType.Integer
  • String types: char, Character, String, StringBuffer, StringBuilder, CharSequence, CharBuffer → PropertyType.String
  • Special types: BigInteger → PropertyType.BigInteger, UUID → PropertyType.UUID
  • URI types: URI, URL → PropertyType.URI

Types not found in this map are handled by additional logic in {@link #typeOf(Class, Type)}.

name: CheapDao
description:
Interface for saving and loading Cheap elements to/from a database. Provides operations to persist the entire Cheap model structure including definitions, entities, aspects, properties, and hierarchies.

This interface supports two persistence strategies for aspects:

  • Default Tables: Uses generic aspect/property_value tables for flexible schema
  • Mapped Tables: Uses custom tables with typed columns for better performance

Implementations must handle transactional integrity, ensuring that catalog saves and loads are atomic operations. Foreign key constraints should be enforced where supported by the underlying database. @see AspectTableMapping @see Catalog @see AbstractCheapDao

name: Aspect
description:
Represents an aspect that can be attached to an entity. An Aspect is a collection of related properties that describe a particular facet or characteristic of an entity.

Aspects are analogous to rows in database terminology or file attributes in a filesystem context. Every aspect has a definition that specifies its structure and the properties it can contain.

Aspects have three lifecycle stages:

  1. Detached (optional): not attached to any entity
  2. Attached: attached to an entity, but not in any catalog
  3. Saved: Attached to an entity and stored in a catalog

This interface provides both safe (type-checked) and unsafe (unchecked) methods for reading and writing property values. Safe methods perform validation against the aspect definition, while unsafe methods bypass validation for performance or when working with dynamic schemas.

Property access is controlled by the aspect definition's security model

name: AspectBuilder
description:
A builder interface for creating Aspect instances using the builder pattern. This interface allows fluent configuration of an aspect's entity, definition, and property values before building the final Aspect instance.

The builder pattern provides a clean and readable way to construct aspects with multiple properties, especially when dealing with complex aspect structures.

Example usage:

{@code
AspectBuilder builder = someImplementation();
Aspect aspect = builder
    .entityId(UUID.randomUUID())
    .aspectDef(myAspectDef)
    .property("name", "John Doe")
    .property("age", 30)
    .build();
}

name: AspectDef
description:
Defines the structure and metadata for an aspect type within the Cheap data model. An aspect definition specifies the properties that can be associated with entities and controls the read/write capabilities and mutability of those properties.

In the Cheap model, aspects represent collections of properties that can be attached to entities, similar to rows in a database table or attributes of a file. The AspectDef serves as the schema definition for these aspects.

name: AspectMap
description: A Map of entities to their aspects of a specific type.

name: AspectMapHierarchy
description:
A hierarchy that maps entities to their aspects of a specific type. This represents the ASPECT_SET (AM) hierarchy type in the Cheap model, providing a direct mapping from entities to their associated aspect instances.

This hierarchy combines the standard hierarchy interface with Map functionality, allowing efficient storage and retrieval of aspects by their associated entities. All aspects in this hierarchy share the same AspectDef, ensuring type consistency.

name: Catalog
description:
Represents a catalog in the Cheap data model, serving as the "C" in the Cheap acronym (Catalog, Hierarchy, Entity, Aspect, Property). A Catalog is the top-level container that organizes and provides access to hierarchies of entities and their aspects.

Catalogs are analogous to databases in traditional database systems or volumes in file systems. They serve as the primary caching layer and provide the organizational structure for all data within their scope.

All catalogs in Cheap are caches by design, with different types (ROOT, MIRROR) determining their relationship to upstream data sources. Catalogs maintain two special hierarchies: one for managing other hierarchies and one for managing aspect definitions.

Catalogs extend Entity, meaning they have their own global identity and can have aspects attached to them, allowing for metadata about the catalog itself.

name: CatalogDef
Entity
description:
Represents an entity in the Cheap data model. An Entity is only a conceptual object. It does not have any specific properties except for a global ID; all other properties are stored in Aspects, which in turn are stored in Catalogs. Entities are not "stored" anywhere, since they have no data. The global ID serves as a key to locate Aspects. Entities are analogous to primary keys in database terminology.

Each Entity has a globally unique identifier (UUID) and can have an arbitrary set of Aspects attached to it - but no more than one of each Aspect type, as defined by an AspectDef.

Implementors of this interface must implement equals() and hashCode() using ONLY the globalId.

Entities are referenced by their Aspects, and also by some types of Hierarchies.

name: EntityDirectoryHierarchy
description:
A hierarchy that maps string keys to entity references, representing the ENTITY_DIR (ED) hierarchy type in the Cheap model. This hierarchy type provides dictionary-like lookups of entities by string identifiers.

This hierarchy combines the standard hierarchy interface with Map functionality, enabling efficient key-based access to entities through string identifiers. It is analogous to a file directory (hence the name) where files are accessed by name, or a database table with a string primary key.

The mapping is one-to-one: each string key maps to at most one entity, though multiple keys can map to the same entity if the implementation permits. The keys are typically meaningful names, identifiers, or paths that provide semantic access to entities.

Common use cases for EntityDirectoryHierarchy include:

  • Named entity registries where entities are accessed by symbolic names
  • Path-based entity lookups (e.g., "/users/john", "confi

name: EntityListHierarchy
description:
A hierarchy that maintains an ordered list of entity references, representing the ENTITY_LIST (EL) hierarchy type in the Cheap model. This hierarchy type allows duplicate entity references and preserves insertion order.

This hierarchy combines the standard hierarchy interface with List functionality, providing indexed access to entities and supporting operations like sequential traversal, positional insertion, and duplicate entries. It is analogous to a database table with no primary key constraint or a file manifest that can contain multiple references to the same file.

Use cases for EntityListHierarchy include:

  • Maintaining processing queues or work lists where entities may appear multiple times
  • Recording event sequences or audit trails where the same entity may be logged repeatedly
  • Preserving ordered collections where position matters (e.g., playlist, ranked lists)
  • Modeling many-to-many relationships with orde

name: EntitySetHierarchy
description:
A hierarchy that maintains a possibly-ordered set of unique entity references, representing the ENTITY_SET (ES) hierarchy type in the Cheap model. This hierarchy type ensures that each entity appears at most once in the collection.

This hierarchy combines the standard hierarchy interface with Set functionality, providing efficient membership testing and automatic duplicate elimination. The set may be ordered (e.g., using a LinkedHashSet implementation) or unordered (e.g., using a HashSet implementation), depending on the specific implementation chosen.

Unlike {@link EntityListHierarchy}, this hierarchy enforces uniqueness of entity references, making it analogous to a database table with a primary key constraint or a collection of unique file references. This makes EntitySetHierarchy ideal for:

  • Maintaining collections of unique entities without duplicates
  • Implementing tag or category memberships where each entity belongs once

name: EntityTreeHierarchy
description:
Represents a tree-structured hierarchy of entities where nodes can have named children and leaf nodes represent actual entities. This is the "ET" (Entity Tree) hierarchy type in the Cheap model.

Tree hierarchies are useful for representing file systems, organizational structures, taxonomies, or any data that has a natural parent-child relationship with named paths to leaf entities.

The tree structure allows for efficient navigation and querying of hierarchical data, with each node potentially containing an entity and supporting named child lookups.

name: Hierarchy
description:
Base interface for all hierarchy types in the Cheap data model. A Hierarchy represents the "H" in the Cheap acronym (Catalog, Hierarchy, Entity, Aspect, Property) and provides organized access to entities within a catalog.

Hierarchies define the structure and organization of entities, serving roles analogous to tables or indexes in database terminology, or directory structures in file systems. Each hierarchy has a specific type that determines its organizational behavior and access patterns.

All hierarchies are associated with a catalog and have a definition that specifies their type, name, and organizational characteristics.

name: HierarchyDef
description:
Defines the metadata and characteristics of a hierarchy within the Cheap data model. A hierarchy definition specifies the type, name, and mutability constraints of a hierarchy instance.

In the Cheap model, hierarchies provide the structural organization for entities and aspects within a catalog. The HierarchyDef serves as the schema definition that determines how a hierarchy behaves and what operations are permitted on it.

name: LocalEntity
description:
LocalEntity keeps track of one or more Catalog(s) that contain its Aspects.
LocalEntity references are therefore sufficient to access Aspects, without
needing a Catalog reference.

name: MutableAspectDef
description:
Defines the structure and metadata for an aspect type within the Cheap data model. An aspect definition specifies the properties that can be associated with entities and controls the read/write capabilities and mutability of those properties.

In the Cheap model, aspects represent collections of properties that can be attached to entities, similar to rows in a database table or attributes of a file. The AspectDef serves as the schema definition for these aspects.

name: Property
description:
Represents an individual, immutable property within an aspect, serving as the "P" in the Cheap acronym (Catalog, Hierarchy, Entity, Aspect, Property). A Property combines a value with its definition and provides type-safe access to the data.

Properties are analogous to columns in database terminology or individual attributes in object-oriented programming. Each property has a definition that specifies its type, constraints, and access permissions.

Properties are immutable once created - their values cannot be changed through this interface. To modify property values, new Property instances must be created and applied to their containing Aspect.

name: PropertyDef
description:
Defines the metadata and constraints for a property within the Cheap data model. A property definition specifies the name, type, and access characteristics of a property that can be associated with aspects.

In the Cheap model, properties represent the atomic units of data, similar to columns in a database table or instance variables in objects. The PropertyDef serves as the schema definition that determines how property values are stored, accessed, and validated.

name: Node
description:
Represents a node in the entity tree hierarchy. Each node can contain an entity and act as a container for named child nodes, implementing the Map interface for efficient child lookup by name.

Nodes can be either leaf nodes (containing entities with no children) or internal nodes (which may contain entities and have child nodes). The tree structure is navigable in both directions through parent-child relationships.

name: CheapRagClient
description:
Client interface for the CHEAP RAG REST API.
Provides methods for querying the RAG system and retrieving index status.

name: CheapRestClient
description:
REST client for interacting with the Cheap REST API.
Provides methods for managing catalogs, aspect definitions, aspects, and hierarchies.

name: TagRegistry
description:
Central registry for managing tag definitions and applications in the CHEAP framework.

The TagRegistry provides a complete API for:

  • Defining and managing tag definitions
  • Applying and removing tags from CHEAP elements
  • Querying tags by element, name, or namespace
  • Validating tag applications
  • Managing tag inheritance hierarchies
  • Initializing standard cheap.core tags

All tag data is stored in CHEAP hierarchies within the provided catalog, using the following storage structure:

  • tag_definitions - AspectMapHierarchy storing all tag definitions
  • tag_applications_* - AspectMapHierarchies for tag applications (one per ElementType)
  • tag_index_by_name - EntityDirectoryHierarchy for fast name lookups
  • tags_by_element - EntityDirectoryHierarchy for fast element lookups

Example usage:

{@code
// C

name: GenericGetterSetter
description:
Generic interface for unified getter and setter operations across different data types.

This interface provides a common abstraction for accessing and modifying object properties through reflection-based operations. It supports both object reference types and all primitive types, allowing for efficient method handle-based property access without the overhead of boxing/unboxing for primitive values.

The interface is designed to work with the {@link ReflectionWrapper} system to create optimized lambda-based implementations that avoid the performance costs of traditional reflection while maintaining type safety through method handle dispatch.

Thread Safety: Implementations are expected to be thread-safe for concurrent read operations, but thread safety for write operations depends on the underlying target object's thread safety characteristics. @see ReflectionWrapper @see GetterSetterSignature @see MethodDef

name: ThrowingGetter
description:
Functional interface for operations that may throw checked exceptions.

This interface enables clean exception handling in lambda expressions where checked exceptions need to be converted to unchecked exceptions. @param the return type of the operation

name: saveAspectDef
saveCatalog
description:
Saves a complete catalog to the database, including all its definitions, entities, aspects, properties, and hierarchies.

This method creates a new database connection and wraps the operation in a transaction. If any part of the save fails, all changes are rolled back.

The save process follows this order:

  1. Save the Catalog entity and catalog record
  2. Save all AspectDefs and link them to the catalog
  3. Save all Hierarchies and their content (entities, aspects, properties)
@param catalog the catalog to save @throws SQLException if database operation fails @throws IllegalArgumentException if catalog is null or has invalid state

description:

This overload accepts a Connection, useful for in-memory SQLite databases where the connection must remain open to prevent database deletion. @param connection the database connection to execute the DDL against @throws SQLException if database operation fails

name: executeMainSchemaDdl
executeMainSchemaDdl
description:
Executes the main Cheap schema DDL script to create all core tables and indexes. This creates the foundation database structure for the Cheap data model.

This overload accepts a Connection, useful for in-memory SQLite databases where the connection must remain open to prevent database deletion. @param connection the database connection to execute the DDL against @throws SQLException if database operation fails

name: executeSqlStatements
description:
Executes multiple SQL statements from a DDL script.
Properly parses SQL statements considering BEGIN...END blocks, comments, and string literals.
This is necessary because SQLite doesn't support executing multiple statements in a single call.

name: executeTruncateSchemaDdl
executeTruncateSchemaDdl
description:
Executes the truncate schema DDL script to delete all data from Cheap tables while preserving the schema structure. This is useful for clearing test data or resetting the database without recreating all tables and constraints.

This overload accepts a Connection, useful for in-memory SQLite databases where the connection must remain open to prevent database deletion. @param connection the database connection to execute the DDL against @throws SQLException if database operation fails

description:
{@inheritDoc}

This implementation caches the computed hash value for improved performance. The cache uses a volatile long variable with 0 as the sentinel value indicating "not yet computed". This is safe because the FNV-1a hash algorithm never produces a hash value of 0 for any non-empty input.

Note: For mutable AspectDef implementations, the cached hash may become stale if properties are added or removed. Subclasses that allow modification should call {@link #invalidateHashCache()} when the aspect definition is modified.

This should be called by mutable subclasses whenever the aspect definition is modified.

nullabilityOf
description:
Determines whether a type can be null based on its class and annotations.

This is the core nullability detection logic used by all other nullability methods. The determination follows these rules:

  1. Primitive types are never nullable (return {@code false})
  2. Types annotated with any {@code @NotNull} annotation are not nullable (return {@code false})
  3. All other reference types are assumed nullable (return {@code true})

Annotation Detection:

The method detects {@code @NotNull} annotations by checking the simple class name, making it compatible with NotNull annotations from multiple libraries (JetBrains, javax.validation, FindBugs, etc.) without requiring specific dependencies.

@param type the Java class to check for nullability @param annotations the annotations to analyze, or null if no annotations @return {@code true} if the type can be null, {@code false} if it cannot be null @throws Null

typeOf
description:
Maps a Java type to the corresponding Cheap {@link PropertyType}.

This is the core type mapping method that handles the conversion from Java's type system to Cheap's property type system. The mapping process follows these steps:

  1. Multivalued type unwrapping: For arrays and Collections, extracts the component type
  2. Direct mapping lookup: Checks {@link #CLASS_PROPERTY_TYPE_MAP} for exact matches
  3. Date/time type detection: Handles Date, Calendar, and TemporalAccessor types
  4. Fallback: Defaults to PropertyType.String for unrecognized types

Multi-valued handling:

For array and Collection types, this method extracts the component type before mapping:

  • Array types: uses {@link Class#getComponentType()}
  • Collection types: uses {@link #getCollectionComponentType} with generic type information

This static factory method uses Java's standard Bean introspection to discover properties and create corresponding {@link PojoPropertyDef} instances. All properties are created with immutable=true, meaning only getter methods are considered.

  3. Forces immutable=true to ignore setter methods

name: remove
setter
fromGetterOnly
description:
Creates a read-only property definition from a getter method.

This factory method analyzes the getter method to automatically derive:

  • Property name by removing "get" or "is" prefix and lowercasing the first letter
  • Property type from the method's return type
  • Nullability from method annotations and return type
  • Multivalued status for arrays and collections
  • Primitive type status for boxing optimization

Method name examples:

  • {@code getName()} → property name "name"
  • {@code isActive()} → property name "active"
  • {@code value()} → property name "value" (no prefix to remove)
@param getter the getter method to analyze @return a read-only property definition based on the getter method @throws NullPointerException if getter is null @throws IllegalArgumentException if getter is not a valid getter method @see CheapReflectionUtil#assertGetter(Method)

name: fromGetterSetter
description:
Creates a read-write property definition from a getter/setter method pair.

This factory method analyzes both methods to create a complete property definition. The property name is derived from the getter method, and type compatibility between the getter return type and setter parameter type is assumed to have been validated.

Property characteristics are derived as follows:

  • Property name from getter method name (removing "get"/"is" prefix)
  • Property type from getter return type
  • Nullability combines both getter and setter annotations (property is non-null if either is marked non-null)
  • Multi-valued status from getter return type
  • Primitive type status from getter return type
@param getter the getter method for reading the property @param setter the setter method for writing the property @return a read-write property definition based on both methods @throws NullPointerException if either gett

name: fromPropertyDescriptor
description:
Creates a property definition from a JavaBean {@link PropertyDescriptor}.

This factory method provides integration with Java's standard introspection mechanism. It analyzes the property descriptor to determine available methods and creates the appropriate property definition based on method availability and mutability settings.

Method selection logic:

  • If immutable=true or no setter available → creates read-only property using {@link #fromGetterOnly}
  • If no getter available → creates write-only property using {@link #fromSetterOnly}
  • If both methods available and immutable=false → creates read-write property using {@link #fromGetterSetter}
@param prop the PropertyDescriptor from Java Bean introspection @param immutable whether to treat the property as immutable (ignore setter even if available) @return a property definition based on the descriptor's methods and mutability setting @throws NullPointerException if p

name: fromSetterOnly
description:
Creates a write-only property definition from a setter method.

This factory method analyzes the setter method to automatically derive:

  • Property name by removing "set" prefix and lowercasing the first letter
  • Property type from the method's parameter type
  • Nullability from method parameter annotations
  • Multivalued status for arrays and collections
  • Primitive type status for boxing optimization

Method name examples:

  • {@code setName(String name)} → property name "name"
  • {@code name(String value)} → property name "name" (no prefix to remove)
@param setter the setter method to analyze @return a write-only property definition based on the setter method @throws NullPointerException if setter is null @throws IllegalArgumentException if setter is not a valid setter method @see CheapReflectionUtil#assertSetter(Method)

name: aspectDefConsistency
description:
Test 2: AspectDef Consistency
Create identical aspect defs via each client and compare JSON responses.

name: catalogStructureConsistency
description:
Test 1: Catalog Structure Consistency
Create identical catalogs via each client and verify responses are structurally identical.

name: paginationConsistency
description:
Test 5: Pagination Consistency
Test pagination with identical parameters across all databases via clients,
verify page structures and content are identical.

name: queryConsistency
description:
Test 4: Query Consistency
Query same data from all three servers via clients and verify JSON responses are identical.

name: sortingOrderingConsistency
description:
Test 6: Sorting/Ordering Consistency
Test ordering with same parameters across all databases via clients,
verify sort order is identical.

name: startAllServers
description:
Start all three database servers before running any tests.
Each server runs on a different port with its own database backend.

name: stopAllServers
description: Stop all three database servers after all tests complete.

name: upsertConsistency
description:
Test 3: Upsert Consistency
Perform identical upsert operations via all three clients and verify success responses are identical.

The aspect definition serves as the schema for this aspect instance, defining what properties are available and their access permissions.

name: entity

name: isTransferable
description:
Returns a flag indicating whether this aspect may be transferred between entities. Defaults to false. If it's false, Cheap will not allow changes to its owning entity.

This flag is not stored on the AspectDef because it is an implementation detail, not metadata.

@return whether this aspect may be transferred between entities

name: readObj
setEntity
description:
Set the entity that owns this aspect. If the entity is already set and this is not flagged as transferable by its AspectDef, an Exception will be thrown.

This method can only modify the aspect itself. It will not invoke the entity's {@link Entity#attach(Aspect) attach} method; that method invokes this one.

@param entity the entity to attach this aspect to, never null

name: uncheckedRead
unsafeAdd
description:
Adds a new property to this aspect without validation. This method bypasses security checks and assumes the aspect definition allows property addition.

Use with caution - this method can violate aspect definition constraints and should only be used when performance is critical and safety is ensured elsewhere.

@param prop the property to add, must not be null

@param propName the name of the property to remove, must not be null

Leaf nodes typically represent terminal entities in the hierarchy, while internal nodes represent containers or directories that can hold other nodes.

@return the entity associated with this node, or null if no entity is attached

jdbcScheduler
description:
Creates a bounded elastic scheduler for blocking database operations.
This scheduler is designed for blocking I/O and won't block the reactive event loop.

The bounded elastic scheduler creates threads on demand up to a configured limit,
and recycles them after a TTL period. This is ideal for wrapping blocking calls
(like JDBC) in a reactive application.

@return a Scheduler instance for blocking operations

getAppliedAt
description:
Returns the timestamp when the tag was applied.

@return the application timestamp (never null)

name: getAppliedBy
description:
Returns the identifier of the user or system that applied the tag.

This might be a username, email address, or system identifier. It may be null if the source is unknown or not tracked.

@return the applier identifier (may be null)

name: getMetadata
description:
Returns the optional tag-specific metadata.

Metadata provides additional context or configuration for the tag application. For example, a "format" tag might include a format pattern, or a "range-bounded" tag might include min/max values.

@return immutable map of metadata (never null, but may be empty)

name: getSource
getTagDefinitionId
description:
Returns the UUID of the tag definition entity being applied.

This references the entity in the tag_definitions hierarchy that contains the TagDefinition aspect.

@return the tag definition entity UUID (never null)

name: getTargetElementId
description:
Returns the UUID of the element being tagged.

This is the global ID of the CHEAP element (Property, Aspect, Entity, Hierarchy, or Catalog) that this tag is attached to.

@return the target element UUID (never null)

name: getTargetType
description:
Returns the type of the element being tagged.

@return the target element type (never null)

name: getAliases
description:
Returns the list of alternative names for this tag.

Aliases provide alternative ways to reference the tag, useful for backward compatibility or alternative naming conventions.

@return immutable list of aliases (never null, but may be empty)

name: getAppliesTo
description:
Returns the list of element types this tag can be applied to.

A tag may be applicable to one or more element types. For example, a "required" tag might apply to both PROPERTY and ASPECT types.

@return immutable list of applicable element types (never null or empty)

name: getDescription
description:
Returns the human-readable description of this tag's purpose and semantics.

Descriptions should clearly explain what the tag means and how it should be used. They are intended for both human developers and LLMs.

@return the tag description (never null)

name: getFullName
description:
Returns the full qualified name of this tag.

The full name is constructed as {@code namespace.name} and uniquely identifies the tag across all namespaces.

@return the full qualified tag name (e.g., "cheap.core.primary-key")

name: getName

loadFileHierarchy
description:
Loads file and directory information into a catalog as both aspects and a hierarchical tree structure.

This method performs a depth-first traversal of the filesystem and creates:

  • {@link RecordAspect} instances for each file/directory (added to the catalog's aspect map)
  • An {@link EntityTreeHierarchy} that mirrors the filesystem directory structure
  • Entities with local aspect maps containing the FileRec information

The tree hierarchy uses regular nodes for directories and leaf nodes for files. Note that empty directories are treated as nodes (not leaves) to allow future child additions.

The catalog must already have a {@link RecordAspectDef} registered for the FileRec type. The hierarchy will be created and registered in the catalog using the provided hierarchyDef. @param catalog the catalog to load the hierarchy into @param hierarchyDef the hierarchy definition for the tree structure @param dir the root directory t

equals
description:
Determines equality based on signature characteristics only.

Two signatures are considered equal if they have the same return type, parameter count, and parameter types. The declaring class is deliberately excluded from the comparison to enable matching methods from different classes that have compatible signatures. @param other the object to compare with this signature @return true if the signatures match (excluding declaring class)

name: from
description:
Creates a normalized signature from a target method for matching against wrapper methods.

This factory method transforms a target getter/setter method signature into a normalized form that can be matched against the {@link GenericGetterSetter} interface methods. The normalization process includes:

  • Adding the receiver object as the first parameter (Object.class)
  • Preserving primitive parameter types for proper overload resolution
  • Converting reference parameter types to Object.class for generic matching
  • Normalizing the return type using {@link #getReturnType(Method)}
@param method the target getter or setter method to normalize @return a normalized signature that can match wrapper interface methods @throws NullPointerException if method is null

name: fromWrapper
getReturnType
description:
Normalizes a method's return type for signature matching purposes.

This utility method performs return type normalization to enable matching between getter/setter methods and their corresponding wrapper interface methods. The normalization rules are:

  • {@code void.class} remains unchanged (for setter methods)
  • All other return types (including primitives) become {@code Object.class} (for getter methods)
@param method the method whose return type should be normalized @return {@code void.class} for void methods, {@code Object.class} for all others @throws NullPointerException if method is null

name: hashCode
description:
Computes hash code based on signature characteristics only.

The hash code calculation includes return type, parameter count, and parameter types, but deliberately excludes the declaring class to enable cross-class signature matching. @return hash code for this signature
